coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional iron-doped carbon dots: Integration of fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging for enhanced photodynamic therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Tongtong
Cao, Lei
Li, Xinlu
Du, Yuwei
Yan, Haiyang
Chang, Zhimin
Dong, Wen-Fei
Li, Li
coredata.dc:description: Multidimensional imaging materials are highly sought after in scientific research and clinical applications due to their ability to provide comprehensive imaging data while minimizing experimental interference. The iron-doped carbon dots (TPFe-CDs) prepared in this study possess dual-mode imaging capabilities, combining fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging, along with photodynamic therapy functionality. In fluorescence imaging, TPFe-CDs exhibit versatility, being effectively excited by either single-photon or two-photon excitation. This versatility enables successful imaging across various biological samples, including cells, organoids, and zebrafish. Moreover, TPFe-CDs demonstrate exceptional three-dimensional imaging capabilities, penetrating depths exceeding 300 µm, thus enabling deeper tissue imaging beyond surface layers. Additionally, TPFe-CDs serve as excellent contrast agents in magnetic resonance imaging, showcasing a T2 relaxation rate of 10.388 mM−1s−1. In mouse models, magnetic resonance imaging revealed potential targeting of TPFe-CDs, primarily accumulating in the thoracic lung region, with minimal distribution observed in the gastrointestinal tract and tail regions. Furthermore, TPFe-CDs exhibit promising photodynamic therapy capabilities, generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon laser irradiation, leading to successful cancer cell eradication in experimental settings. The multifunctional nature of TPFe-CDs, with their dual-modal imaging capabilities and therapeutic potential, is garnering significant attention and is poised to make substantial contributions to diverse fields such as biological imaging and clinical therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence imaging
Magnetic resonance imaging
Two-photon imaging
Photodynamic therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Contrast medium and MR imaging effects of TPFe-CDs
Mechanism analysis of TPFe-CDs entering cells
Contrast agent and magnetic resonance imaging of the TPFe-CDs
Photodynamic therapeutic effects of the TPFe-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM images of the synthesized TPFe-CDs in experiments. (B) The XRD of the synthesized TPFe-CDs in experiments. (C) The XPS of the synthesized TPFe-CDs in experiments. (D) The FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized TPFe-CDs in experiments. (E) The UV absorption of synthesized TPFe-CDs in experiments. (F) Excitation and emission spectra of synthesized TPFe-CDs. (G) Fluorescence emission spectra of TPFe-CDs under 300–370 nm. (H) Fluorescence emission spectra of TPFe-CDs under 650–750 nm.

(A) One-photon fluorescence imaging results of TPFe-CDs in HeLa, embryo and larvae, respectively. (B) Photographs of TPFe-CDs taken at different penetration depths in the organoids. (a-f) are the images of each layer and (g) is the results after the superposition. The scale bar has been marked separately in the figure.

(A) Two-photon fluorescence imaging results and 3D surface plot of TPFe-CDs in HeLa, embryo and larvae, respectively. (B) Photographs of TPFe-CDs taken at different penetration depths in the zebrafish. (a-h) are the images of each layer and (i) is the results after the superposition.

Experiments on the entry of TPFe-CDs into cells.(c-f) Effects of endocytosis inhibitors (10 mg mL−1 amiloride, 50 mg mL−1 genistein, 5 mg mL−1 MβCD and 5 mg mL−1CPZ for two hour) with 40 mg mL−1 TPFe-CDs, respectively. (B) Average fluorescence intensities of TPFe-CDs (n=3). The scale bar is 50 µm.

(A) The T2 relaxation intensity of the TPFe-CDs changes with the concentration. (B) The linear relationship between the concentration of TPFe-CDs and the relaxation is plotted from the results. Showed a good linear relationship.

MR imaging effects of TPFe-CDs in animals.(A-C) TPFe-CDs detected significant MR signals in the chest and lung regions of mice, but no significant distribution in the gastrointestinal region and tail. (D)This software was used to quantify the ROI selected from three sets of MR images.

Confocal images taken after irradiation by adding TPFe-CDs with DCFH-DA to the cells and placed under single-and two-photon lasers. ROSUP is a commercial reagent and is used to produce ROS as a control. The scale bar is 50 µm.

After photodynamic treatment, the cells were stained by adding calcein-AM and PI to the cells to observe their survival status. Two sets of experiments were performed under single-photon and two-photon laser irradiation. The scale bar is 75 µm.

Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of TPFe-CDs. It has three major functions, namely, fluorescence imaging, magnetic resonance imaging, and photodynamic therapy. The excellent characteristics of this multifunctional carbon point has many applications in many fields.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional iron-doped carbon dots: Integration of fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging for enhanced photodynamic therapy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Science (CAS)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multidimensional imaging materials are highly sought after in scientific research and clinical applications due to their ability to provide comprehensive imaging data while minimizing experimental interference. The iron-doped carbon dots (TPFe-CDs) prepared in this study possess dual-mode imaging capabilities, combining fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging, along with photodynamic therapy functionality. In fluorescence imaging, TPFe-CDs exhibit versatility, being effectively excited by either single-photon or two-photon excitation. This versatility enables successful imaging across various biological samples, including cells, organoids, and zebrafish. Moreover, TPFe-CDs demonstrate exceptional three-dimensional imaging capabilities, penetrating depths exceeding 300 µm, thus enabling deeper tissue imaging beyond surface layers. Additionally, TPFe-CDs serve as excellent contrast agents in magnetic resonance imaging, showcasing a T2 relaxation rate of 10.388 mM−1s−1. In mouse models, magnetic resonance imaging revealed potential targeting of TPFe-CDs, primarily accumulating in the thoracic lung region, with minimal distribution observed in the gastrointestinal tract and tail regions. Furthermore, TPFe-CDs exhibit promising photodynamic therapy capabilities, generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon laser irradiation, leading to successful cancer cell eradication in experimental settings. The multifunctional nature of TPFe-CDs, with their dual-modal imaging capabilities and therapeutic potential, is garnering significant attention and is poised to make substantial contributions to diverse fields such as biological imaging and clinical therapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of TPFe-CDs. It has three major functions, namely, fluorescence imaging, magnetic resonance imaging, and photodynamic therapy. The excellent characteristics of this multifunctional carbon point has many applications in many fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular imaging techniques have become indispensable tools in modern biological research, enabling researchers to visualize and study the distribution, structure, and function of biomolecules within cells, tissues, and organisms [1,2] . These techniques offer opportunities for in-depth understanding of biological processes and disease mechanisms [3]. Molecular imaging encompasses various modalities, including mass spectrometry imaging [4], optical coherence imaging [5], radioactive isotope imaging [6], magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [7], conventional fluorescence imaging [8], and two-photon imaging [9], among others. However, each molecular imaging technique has its unique advantages and inherent limitations, making it challenging to obtain accurate and reliable information in specific research areas or physiological structures [10]. For instance, conventional fluorescence imaging, based on the principles of fluorescent molecular labeling, boasts high sensitivity, the ability for multi-color imaging, and real-time imaging capabilities [11]. Nevertheless, it is limited by depth imaging constraints, the risk of photodamage, and background autofluorescence [12]. Two-photon imaging, on the other hand, is an advanced microscopy technique that overcomes some limitations of traditional fluorescence imaging by simultaneously using two photons to excite fluorescent markers within the sample [13]. It enables high-resolution imaging in deep tissues, reduces the risk of photodamage, and provides higher spatial resolution [14]. However, it requires highly specialized equipment and expertise, comes at a relatively higher cost, and relies on specific fluorescent markers. MRI is a non-invasive imaging technique that utilizes the principles of nuclear magnetic resonance to generate high-resolution images [15]. It offers advantages such as radiation-free imaging, deep imaging capabilities, and multi-parameter information [16]. Nevertheless, MRI scans typically take longer, are more expensive, and have lower spatial resolution compared to microscopy techniques. To overcome these inherent limitations, there is an urgent need for a multimodal imaging technology that can effectively combine the strengths of different techniques, providing more powerful molecular imaging capabilities [17]. For example, combining conventional fluorescence imaging, two-photon imaging, and magnetic resonance imaging can achieve high-sensitivity, radiation-free deep imaging across a variety of biological samples, greatly expanding imaging capabilities and application scenarios [18].

Initially, CDs were studied as emerging forms of carbon materials, with relatively simple preparation methods often involving the thermal treatment or microwave irradiation of carbon sources like sucrose or glucose [19]. However, these materials quickly gained widespread interest due to their excellent fluorescence properties, making them hot topics of research in the fields of biological imaging and labeling [20]. In the field of fluorescence imaging, CDs have become highly regarded optical probes due to their unique characteristics [21]. Their biocompatibility and low toxicity make them ideal choices for labeling biological samples [22]. CDs can be introduced into living cells and organisms as fluorescent markers, allowing efficient visualization, tracking, and study of various biomolecules within cells, including proteins, nucleic acids, and subcellular structures [23]. Advances in this field primarily focus on developing more sensitive, stable, and multifunctional CDs to meet diverse biological imaging needs [24].

The field of two-photon imaging has also benefited from CD research, as CDs are widely used in this area, mainly due to their nonlinear two-photon absorption properties [25]. This attribute enables CDs to achieve high-resolution imaging in deep tissues, particularly in the fields of neuroscience research and in vivo imaging, showing outstanding performance [26]. Recent research suggests that CDs also hold potential in the field of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [27]. By functionalizing CDs with magnetic properties, they can be used as MRI contrast agents, enhancing the prospects of MRI in biomedical research and clinical diagnostics [28]. Progress in this field implies that CDs can provide high-contrast images for MRI, allowing better visualization of tissue structures and biological processes [29]. However, despite remarkable advancements in multiple imaging fields using CDs, to the best of our knowledge, there has not yet been a CD that combines the three-modal imaging capabilities of conventional fluorescence imaging, two-photon imaging, and magnetic resonance imaging. Furthermore, many CDs with multimodal imaging capabilities are achieved through post-synthesis modifications and have not achieved one-step synthesis of multimodal imaging-capable CDs.

This study demonstrates the successful achievement of fluorescence imaging under both one-photon and two-photon conditions by incorporating Fe carbon dots (TPFe-CDs), which can be utilized for magnetic resonance imaging in mice ( Scheme 1). Fluorescence imaging has proven effective across various biological samples, including cells, organoids, and zebrafish. During magnetic resonance imaging of mice, carbon dots accumulate in the thoracic lung region and undergo gradual metabolism over time, indicating potential targeting capabilities and opening up new avenues for detecting thoracic lung diseases. Moreover, TPFe-CDs exhibit promising photodynamic therapeutic effects, effectively eradicating cancer cells under laser irradiation in both single- and two-photon conditions. Hence, the exceptional multi-mode imaging capabilities and potent photodynamic therapeutic properties of TPFe-CDs hold significant promise for applications across numerous fields.

Trisodium citrate, ethylene glycol, stir vigorously, and FeCl3 were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. In the course of the experiment, by precision weighing and dissolving the chemical reagents, and then the solvent was added to prepare a specific concentration of solution, and the deionized water was used to ensure the accuracy of the experimental results.

Dissolve 2 g of trisodium citrate in 30 mL of ethylene glycol with 1 g FeCl3 for vigorous stirring. After complete dissolution, 2 mL of ethylenediamine was added, and the reaction was performed at 200℃ for 8 h. The resulting dark liquid was rotary-evaporated and diluted by adding deionized water. Finally, the solid was configured as a fixed concentration of carbon point solution for subsequent experiments.

For TPFe-CDs research, a variety of analytical techniques were used, including transmission electron microscope (TEM) observation, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy measurement, fluorescence spectroscopy detection, and ultraviolet visible absorption spectroscopy measurement. Specifically, after TPFe-CDs was dissolved in ethanol, a UV–visible absorption spectrometer and a fluorescence spectrometer were used to record the absorption and emission spectra. The experimental data included full spectral scans and the International Lighting Committee (CIE) results obtained by software analysis. TP images were acquired with the Olympus BX61 with a 20X water objective (Olympus, Japan), which was observed under femtosecond pulse excitation at 808 nm.

Complete medium was prepared using the recommended ratio combination of FBS and DMEM, with HeLa cells selected as a research focus. The TPFe-CDs was added to adherent cells for 1 h, then cells were washed three times with PBS and subjected to confocal imaging. The concentration of TPFe-CDs was uniformly maintained at 40 μg/mL in all fluorescence imaging experiments. Single-photon imaging used excitation at 365 nm and two-photon excitation at 808 nm. Culture time in organoids was 12 h. Zebrafish embryos and larvae provided by Nanjing EzeRinka Biotechnology Co., Ltd.Zebrafish were cultured at 28.5℃, the optimal breeding condition. All experiments were performed in accordance with institutional ethics committee regulations and animal welfare guidelines. Inhibitors of four endocytic pathways (CPZ, genistein, M β CD and amiloride) were used to study the entry of carbon entry into cells. Confocal images were taken using the software and analyzed for the brightness of their fluorescence. These experiments were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contrast medium and MR imaging effects of TPFe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of TPFe-CDs were configured to observe their effect as contrast agent and to plot the longitudinal relaxation rate (1/T2) versus Fe concentration were judged to be available for T2-weighted MR imaging. The prepared contrast solution was injected into mice by tail intravenous injection and placed in the MR equipment, and MR images of different parts were taken at 0,10,30,45,60,90 min. Signal intensity was processed by software and plotted as bar graphs. The experimental equipment is MesoMR23–060H-I medium-sized nuclear magnetic resonance imaging analyzer, produced by Suzhou Numai Analytical Instrument Co., LTD. The resonance frequency is 21 MHz, the magnet strength is 0.5 T, the coil diameter is 60 mm, and the magnet temperature is 32℃.Healthy 4-week-old female BALB/c mice were used. All animal experiments were approved by the medical ethics committee.

DCFH-DA was used to detect the production of intracellular reactive oxygen species, and the commercial reagent ROSUP was used as a control. HeLa The cells were cultured for 24 hours with safe concentrations of TPFe-CDs and placed under a 365 nm or 808 nm laser for duration consistent with intensity. The results of the fluorescence images were taken under confocal by staining the cells using calcein-AM and PI.

Multiple methods were used for the characterization of the TPFe-CDs. As shown in the Fig. 1A, from the TEM results, the size of TPFe-CDs is about 5 nm, and it is spherical-like, consistent with the category of carbon nanodots [30]. The XRD results showed that TPFe-CDs have a distinct peak at 22°, which coincides with the structure of graphene (1,0,1), indicating that carbon nanodot materials have been successfully prepared (Fig. 1B). Further, XPS and FTIR were used to analyze the elemental composition and bearing groups of TPFe-CDs. As shown in the Fig. 1 C, TPFe-CDs mainly contain four elements, C, N, O and N, accounting for 63.64 %, 15.83 %, 16.02 % and 0.25 %, which are consistent with the element species contained in the precursor, and a small amount of Na and Cl, which are residues of the precursor. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Figure S1A) corresponds to the C-O bond at 284.43 eV, the C-C bond at 285.18 eV, and the OC-O bond at 287.69 eV. Similarly, the N 1 s spectrum (Figure S1B) contains two peaks of 399.11 and 400.33 eV that can correspond to the C-N and C-NH2 bonds, respectively. Lattice oxygen and adsorbed oxygen correspond to 530.65 and 531.50 eV in the O 1 s spectrum (Figure S1C). Finally, the Fe2p spectrum can observe four peaks of 710.06, 716.05, 723.8 and 730.31 eV (Figure S1D). FT-IR was used to characterize the structural features and functional groups of TPFe-CDs. As shown in the Fig. 1D, 3435 cm−1 corresponds to C-H expansion, 2924 cm−1 to C-H expansion, 1637 cm−1 and 1386 cm−1 to COO- in sodium citrate. Also, in the meantime, the absorption peak at 1058 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C-OH, possibly from ethylene glycol in the reaction system. These results show that carbon nanodot materials with iron are successfully synthesized, and the surface is rich in various functional groups, providing a solid foundation for further material applications [31].

The optical properties of the TPFe-CDs have been intensively studied. As shown in the Fig. 1E, TPFe-CDs have a distinct UV absorption peak at 350 nm, which corresponds to the characteristic peak of the n-π* transition of the base. After testing by the spectrofluorometer, it was concluded that the optimal excitation of TPFe-CDs is 370 nm and the optimal emission is 450 nm (Fig. 1F). The Commission International de L′Eclairage (CIE) coordinates (0.1532, 0.1298) shows the standard blue fluorescence (Figure S2A). Moreover, the absolute quantum efficiency of TPFe-CDs is as high as 34.49 %, which provides a guarantee for the emission of bright fluorescence under low concentration conditions (Figure S2B). The data from the 3 D spectra show that TPFe-CDs have two distinct excitation regions (Figure S2C), suggestive of its two-photon imaging capability. Therefore, light at 300–370 nm and 650–750 nm wavelengths was used to perform excitation TPFe-CDs to verify their performance. The results show that light in both bands produces bright blue fluorescence and stabilized emission wavelength at 450 nm (Fig. 1G, H). This suggests that TPFe-CDs have single-and two-photon imaging capabilities and stable excitation-independent properties. Excitation-independent properties guarantee the stability of the fluorescence emission of TPFe-CDs and accurate results in a wide range of excitation bands. The ability of multiphoton imaging, without a doubt, brings about deeper penetration depths and less light damage, while also facilitating optical tomography. Thus, based on these excellent optical properties, TPFe-CDs have a great potential for fluorescence imaging in living organisms.

Thanks to their unique optical properties, TPFe-CDs exhibit excellent fluorescence imaging performance. First, we explored the fluorescence imaging ability of TPFe-CDs under conventional single-photon excitation. HeLa cells and zebrafish were incubated with TPFe-CDs and the laser confocal results were taken, as shown in the Fig. 2 A. In cells, TPFe-CDs are uniformly distributed and exhibit bright blue fluorescence that is clearly distinguishable from the background. Whether in zebrafish embryos or larvae, TPFe-CDs can pass through the membrane surface and enter the body, and the imaging effect is clear and clear. At the same time, it can be seen that the yolk sac is the main area where TPFe-CDs accumulate, which indicates that carbon dots may enter the zebrafish through endocytosis in yolk sac cells or developing embryos. Organoids are cells cultured in a 3D environment that can simulate organ function, composition and developmental characteristics and play an important role in cancer research, drug screening and other fields. However, currently, there are few nanomaterials available for fluorescence imaging of organoids. In this experiment, we made a bold attempt using TPFe-CDs. Using laser confocal tomography technology, we captured fluorescence images after incubation of carbon dots and organoids. The results are shown in Fig. 2B. At different heights from 100 to 350 µm, TPFe-CDs effectively penetrated into the organoids instead of just staying on the surface. After superposition on the Z-axis, the overall distribution of fluorescence imaging within the organoid can be obtained, which provides a new perspective for fluorescence nanoimaging of organoids.

In the analysis of optical properties, we have discovered the attractive two-photon imaging potential of TPFe-CDs. Through experiments, we further confirmed its capabilities. Using an 800 nm laser as the excitation light source, two-photon fluorescence imaging photos of TPFe-CDs were taken. Fig. 3 A shows that the carbon dots outline clear outlines of cells and zebrafish. Combined with the 3D surface plot results, it can be seen that there is a significant brightness difference between the fluorescence accumulation area and the shooting background. Further, the excellent imaging depth of the two-photon is also reflected in the zebrafish (Fig. 3B). Similar to the results of the single-photon experiment, the carbon dots still accumulated in the zebrafish yolk sac. Similarly, it can be considered that the excellent one-photon and two-photon fluorescence imaging properties of TPFe-CDs can provide strong support for the observation of cells, organoids, zebrafish and other biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism analysis of TPFe-CDs entering cells
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the excellent fluorescence imaging effects of TPFe-CDs, the mechanism of TPFe-CDs entering cells was further studied ( Fig. 4). First, we examined whether TPFe-CDs require energy to enter cells. Incubating cells at 4°C for two hours can reduce intracellular ATP levels. Comparing with the results at 37°C, it can be seen that the fluorescence brightness of TPFe-CDs in cells decreased by 82.5 %, indicating that TPFe-CDs rely on energy to enter cells. Furthermore, four inhibitors were added to the cellular environment to explore the effects of different pathways on the entry of TPFe-CDs into cells. Specifically, chlorpromazine (CPZ) was utilized to inhibit grid-mediated endocytosis, methyl-b-cyclodextrin (MβCD) was used to inhibit lipid-mediated endocytosis, genistein (Gen) was employed to inhibit vesicle-mediated endocytosis, and amiloride (AMI). It can be seen that CPZ has the most significant impact on whether TPFe-CDs can enter cells, followed by AMI. Gen and MβCD did not show obvious inhibition. This indicates that TPFe-CDs are likely to enter cells through clathrin-mediated endocytosis [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contrast agent and magnetic resonance imaging of the TPFe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the one-step synthesis method of TPFe-CDs, iron elements that can enhance the contrast in MRI images are added to the carbon dots, providing support for the realization of magnetic resonance imaging. In magnetic resonance imaging, relaxation refers to the recovery process of the nuclear spin under the influence of an external magnetic field, that is, the process in which the nuclear spin returns to an equilibrium state after being excited. This process involves two main time constants: T1 (longitudinal relaxation time) and T2 (transverse relaxation time), which control how quickly the nuclear spins return to equilibrium and lose phase consistency, respectively. As shown in the Fig. 5 A, it can be seen that as the iron concentration decreases, the T2-weighted MR images become brighter, indicating its concentration-dependent Mr contrast effect. In addition, we also plotted the relationship between the longitudinal relaxation rate (1/T2) and the Fe concentration. As shown in the Fig. 5B, it can be clearly seen that the experimental results are in line with a linear relationship. After fitting with the software, the relaxation rate can be obtained as 10.388 mM−1s−1. Therefore, TPFe-CDs can be used for T2-weighted Mr imaging [33].

The magnetic resonance imaging effect of TPFe-CDs in animals was further studied. The prepared contrast agent solution was injected into the mouse through intravenous injection, and magnetic resonance images of different parts of the mouse were taken at different times. As shown in the Fig. 6, TPFe-CDs detected obvious magnetic resonance signals in the chest and lung areas, but had no obvious distribution in the gastrointestinal tract area and tail area. The software was used to quantitatively analyze the ROIs selected from three sets of magnetic resonance images. The results showed that the brightness in the gastrointestinal tract and tail regions had no obvious fluctuations, while the brightness in the chest and lung regions first increased and then decreased, and reached the peak at 45 minutes. peak. Therefore, we speculate that TPFe-CDs are not dispersed in various organs of mice, but have certain targeting properties to the chest and lung areas. After reaching the peak value, the carbon dots will gradually metabolize in the chest and lungs over time, and the brightness will gradually decrease. These results indicate that TPFe-CDs have the ability to perform magnetic resonance imaging in animals and show potential targeting, providing new possibilities for the identification of thoracic and lung diseases [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodynamic therapeutic effects of the TPFe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A variety of experimental methods have been used to verify the photodynamic therapy effect of TPFe-CDs. The key to achieving PDT is the production of intracellular ROS. Here, we used the DCFH-DA method to detect ROS in HeLa cells under single- and two-photon excitation. DCFH-DA itself has no fluorescence and can freely pass through the cell membrane. After entering the cell, it can be hydrolyzed by intracellular esterases to generate DCFH. Reactive oxygen species in cells can oxidize non-fluorescent DCFH to generate fluorescent DCF. By detecting the fluorescence of DCF, the level of intracellular reactive oxygen species can be known. After HeLa cells were incubated with DCFH-DA, TPFe-CDs or ROSUP were added, and the fluorescence intensity under single-photon or two-photon excitation was detected using a confocal microscope. As shown in the Fig. 7, bright green fluorescence is observed under both single-photon and two-photon laser irradiation, and the effect is similar to the commercial positive reagent ROSUP. In contrast, there was no obvious fluorescence in cells without TPFe-CDs. This indicates that TPFe-CDs can powerfully generate intracellular ROS under single-photon and two-photon laser irradiation, which has great potential to further achieve efficient PDT.

Encouraged by the above results, we used a WST-1-based assay to evaluate the cytotoxicity of TPFe-CDs with and without laser irradiation. As shown in the Figure S3, when the concentration of TPFe-CDs is 0–100 μg/mL, the cell viability of the one-photon and two-photon laser irradiation are both greater than 90 %, indicating that the impact of laser irradiation on cytotoxicity is negligible. The potential effects of temperature changes from laser irradiation or other causes on TPFe-CDs was also investigated. The TPFe-CDs solution temperature was controlled within 10–60℃, and the temperature influence was observed by measuring the fluorescence intensity. The experimental results show that TPFe-CDs maintain good fluorescence stability within 10–60℃, which is crucial for practical applications. Furthermore, to evaluate the photodynamic cell killing effect of TPFe-CDs, we conducted live/dead experiments using HeLa cells, using PI and calcein-AM to distinguish dead cells (red) and live cells (green). As shown in the Fig. 8, when TPFe-CDs were directly observed after incubation with HeLa cells, only green fluorescence was seen, and dead cells stained with red fluorescence by PI were not observed, indicating that the toxicity of TPFe-CDs is negligible in the absence of laser irradiation. When irradiated with single photons or two photons, bright red fluorescence can be observed, indicating that the cells have died. A small amount of green fluorescence may be calcein-AM remaining in the cells. These results indicate that TPFe-CDs have good ROS generation ability and can achieve good photodynamic therapy effects under single-photon or two-photon laser irradiation, providing a new idea for clinical treatment.

In this study, TPFe-CDs have been introduced as a novel and versatile dual-mode imaging probe, demonstrating their exceptional potential in advancing fluorescence imaging, magnetic resonance imaging, and photodynamic therapy. The exploration of their optical properties has revealed promising imaging results under both single- and two-photon excitation, with equally satisfactory outcomes in 3D imaging. The validity of fluorescence imaging results has been successfully confirmed across multiple biological samples, including cells and zebrafish, thereby expanding the scope of their application. Moreover, through a series of meticulously designed experiments, we have elucidated that the cellular uptake of carbon dots is energy-dependent, with clathrin-mediated endocytosis identified as the primary mechanism. These mechanistic insights deepen our understanding of cellular interactions and contribute to the effective utilization of TPFe-CDs in imaging applications. Furthermore, the doping of iron has revitalized the carbon dots, enhancing their contrast effect with concentration gradients and exhibiting a phenomenon of T2 relaxation that lays a solid foundation for MR imaging. Upon entry into the body, TPFe-CDs selectively cluster in the thoracic lung region of mice, indicating potential targeting properties. Lastly, TPFe-CDs have demonstrated promising photodynamic therapeutic effects, efficiently eradicating cancer cells under laser irradiation, and exhibiting favorable outcomes under both single-photon and two-photon conditions. The breakthrough dual-mode imaging carbon dots hold immense promise at the forefront of nanomaterials, poised to make a significant contribution to the fields of biological imaging and clinical therapy.

Yuwei Du: Supervision, Resources, Methodology. Lei Cao: Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization. Tongtong Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wen-Fei Dong: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Li Li: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation. Zhimin Chang: Resources, Methodology. Haiyang Yan: Software, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (2022YFC2403500), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82172077, 22201298, 82071066, and 62027825), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2022QB077 and ZR2022QB243), and the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team of Jiangsu Province (JSSCTD202145).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136812.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular imaging in living subjects: seeing fundamental biological processes in a new light

Ntziachristos, V. Fluorescence molecular imaging. Annu. Rev. Biomed. Eng. 2006, 8, 1-33.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Segal, E.; Friedman, N.; Kaminski, N.; Regev, A.; Koller, D. From signatures to models: understanding cancer using microarrays. Nature genetics 2005, 37 (Suppl 6), S38-S45.

Journal of Alloys and Compounds 2020, 834, 154399.
Zhou, Y.; Zhang, W.; Leblanc, R.M. Structure–property–activity relationships in carbon dots. The Journal of Physical Chemistry B 2022, 126 (51), 10777-10796.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yan, G.-H.; Song, Z.-M.; Liu, Y.-Y.; Su, Q.; Liang, W.; Cao, A.; Sun, Y.-P.; Wang, H. Effects of carbon dots surface functionalities on cellular behaviors–Mechanistic exploration for opportunities in manipulating uptake and translocation. Colloids and Surfaces B: Biointerfaces 2019, 181, 48-57.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, H.; Shen, J.; Li, Y.; Wei, Z.; Cao, G.; Gai, Z.; Hong, K.; Banerjee, P.; Zhou, S. Magnetic iron oxide–fluorescent carbon dots integrated nanoparticles for dual-modal imaging, near-infrared light-responsive drug carrier and photothermal therapy. Biomaterials science 2014, 2 (6), 915-923.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mn2+ complex-modified polydopamine-and dual emissive carbon dots based nanoparticles for in vitro and in vivo trimodality fluorescent, photothermal, and magnetic resonance imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, M.; Zheng, T.; Sheng, B.; Wu, F.; Zhang, Q.; Wang, W.; Shen, J.; Zhou, N.; Sun, Y. Mn2+ complex-modified polydopamine-and dual emissive carbon dots based nanoparticles for in vitro and in vivo trimodality fluorescent, photothermal, and magnetic resonance imaging. Chemical Engineering Journal 2019, 373, 1054-1063.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tongtong Zhu is currently a doctoral student at CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology (SIBET), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China. His research interests focus on fluorescent chemosensors (CDs).

Lei Cao received his Ph.D. degree from University of Science and Technology of China in 2023. He is presently a postdoctor in Institute of Biomedical Health Technology and Engineering, Shenzhen Bay Laboratory, Shenzhen, PR China. His research interests focus on fluorescent chemosensors (CDs).

Xinlu Li is currently a master student at CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology (SIBET), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China. His research interests focus on fluorescent chemosensors (CDs).

Yuwei Du is currently a doctoral student at CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology (SIBET), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China. His research interests focus on fluorescent chemosensors (CDs).

Haiyang Yan is currently a doctoral student at CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology (SIBET), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China. His research interests focus on fluorescent chemosensors (CDs).

Zhimin Chang is mainly engaged in research on nanomedicine and nanobiomedical detection, and has successively proposed a series of innovative results in the aspects of multifunctional diagnosis and treatment integrated nanocarriers, rare cell precision manipulation technology, and ultra-sensitive biological detection technology.

Wen-Fei Dong received his Ph.D. degree in Physical Chemistry in 2004, from the Department of interfaces, Max-Planck-Institute of Colloids and Interfaces, Germany. Now he is a Research Fellow in CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology (SIBET), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China.

Li Li received his Bachelor’s degree in Chemistry in 2009, and his Ph.D. degree in Physical Chemistry in 2014, from Jilin University, PR China. He is interested in photophysics of dye molecules, fluorescence resonance energy, and sensors. Now, he is an Associate Professor in CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology (SIBET), Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China.
